topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box was a monstrosity in the corner of the attic, a forgotten relic from a decade past. Liam shoved it aside with a frustrated grunt, the air thick with dust motes dancing in the single shaft of sunlight. Inside, nestled among yellowed construction paper, was a meticulously organized collection of every graded paper from his entire middle school career. His jaw tightened. He picked up a crumpled essay from seventh grade, the ink faded, and felt a hot flush creep up his neck. "What in the world is this?" he muttered, his voice tight. He flipped through pages, his fingers trembling slightly as he saw the sheer volume of work his mother had preserved. A deep, simmering resentment began to bubble up, a pressure behind his eyes that made his temples throb. He slammed the box shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet space. This felt like an invasion, a deliberate hoarding of his academic life.

[story 2]

Sarah stared at the overflowing filing cabinet in the spare room. It was a mountain of papers, each one a testament to years of diligent effort, neatly categorized by subject and date. A sharp intake of breath escaped her lips. She ran a hand through her hair, the gesture jerky and agitated. "Seriously?" she whispered, the sound brittle in the silence. She pulled out a test from sophomore year, the handwriting shockingly neat. A feeling of profound injustice washed over her, heavy and suffocating. She wanted to tear the whole collection apart, to make a statement about the sheer, overwhelming accumulation of it all. Her knuckles were white as she gripped the edges of the papers, her breathing becoming shallow and rapid. Every page seemed to scream a silent accusation.

[story 3]

Mark found the storage bin tucked beneath the eaves. It was overflowing with homework, report cards, and essays—a complete archive of his scholastic endeavors. He didn't bother with finesse; he just yanked the lid open. The sheer quantity hit him like a physical blow, making him stumble back a step. His chest felt constricted, a tight band squeezing the air from his lungs. He looked down at the stack, his gaze sweeping over the evidence of his past efforts, and a deep, churning dissatisfaction settled in his gut. He let out a low, guttural sound, a sound born of intense vexation. He started pacing the small room, his movements sharp and jerky, unable to settle his gaze on any single document.
